findings.title: Run a structured onboarding call covering 6 key areas before going live
findings.claim: A thorough onboarding call is the single most important trust-building moment and should cover mindset, content expectations, timeline, invoicing, credentials, and Q&A.
findings.what_they_do: They schedule a dedicated onboarding call and systematically cover: (1) model's mindset and expectations, (2) content volume needed upfront and why, (3) realistic 3-month results timeline, (4) invoicing schedule explanation, (5) credential handover for socials and OF, (6) open Q&A floor for the model. They treat the call as a formal first impression, not a casual chat.
findings.vs_us: Palm's onboarding is a form wizard (name, email, commission %, contract, voice memo) per §6 — it is async and document-driven. There is no documented structured live onboarding call. The form captures admin data but does not walk the creator through expectations, timelines, or content strategy.
findings.recommendation: Add a mandatory video onboarding call as Step 2 after the contract wizard. Build a call checklist (Notion/Airtable template) covering the 6 areas above. Record it for reference. This directly addresses the creator-trust gap implied by §6's thin onboarding and supports retention (§9).

sources.quote: schedule that initial onboarding call and run it with the model this is that first impression
you need to establish some sort of a timeline and expectations as to when they should be able to receive the results

findings.title: Build a model profile doc with chatting style, persona facts, and content inventory for chatters
findings.claim: A documented model profile — covering chatting tone, physical stats, hobbies, and personality details — is essential so chatters can maintain consistent fan relationships without breaking immersion.
findings.what_they_do: For every creator they build a profile that includes: existing chatting style (especially for account transitions), height, age, bra size, feet size, astrological sign, hobbies, and other facts that come up naturally in DM conversations. Chatters reference this doc before and during conversations. Content is stored in a shared Dropbox/Google Drive the model can also upload to directly.
findings.vs_us: Palm's external chat team operates without a documented Palm-side model profile SOP (§8 — outsourced, no internal SOP). Fan Tracker CRM (§9) collects fan data but there is no equivalent creator-side profile that briefs chatters on the model's persona and voice.
findings.recommendation: Create a 'Model Brief' Airtable record linked to each creator in the Creators table. Fields: chatting tone/style notes, physical stats, hobbies, boundaries, content categories available. Share with external chat team now; becomes the foundation for in-house chatter onboarding when that roadmap item ships (§8 build-toward).

sources.quote: you need to understand their chatting style if especially if they're running their existing of account and you need to transition their chatting style to your Chatters
little things like their height age bra size feet size astrological sign hobbies things that just naturally get brought up during these conversations

findings.title: Request a specific content mix upfront: sexting sets, PPV bundles, mass-message clips, and verification posts
findings.claim: Agencies must tell models exactly what content types they need at onboarding — not generically 'more content' — so all traffic and monetization strategies can be activated immediately.
findings.what_they_do: During the onboarding call they specify four distinct content buckets needed upfront: (1) sexting/script sets for DM selling, (2) PPV bundles and standalone videos for mass messages, (3) SFW promotional content for Reddit, Twitter, and Instagram, (4) verification posts for subreddits that require them. They also provide a weekly content schedule so the model knows what to film the following week.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is inspo-driven and strong for social/SFW content. However §2 flags that OF vault/PPV content has no systematic production pipeline. There is no documented process for requesting specific content buckets at onboarding or providing weekly content briefs to creators.
findings.recommendation: Add a content-brief template to the onboarding call checklist: enumerate the four buckets (sexting sets, PPV bundles, SFW promo, verification posts) and send a weekly 'content request' message to each creator. This closes the §2 OF-vault gap and connects to Palm's existing social pipeline for the SFW bucket.

sources.quote: scripts so like live content that you could sell via sexting sets in the DMS for of you also need to have ppv bundles and and videos that you can use to mass message out
providing some sort of content schedule to them on a weekly basis as to what you would likely need for that following week

findings.title: Coach creators on 4 non-negotiable quality standards: lighting, audio, background, and variety
findings.claim: Agencies should proactively coach creators on production quality during onboarding rather than waiting for bad content to arrive and damage relationships.
findings.what_they_do: They explicitly advise creators on: (1) lighting — minimum ring light or studio light for long-form video, softer/natural for sexting sets to avoid an overly-produced look; (2) audio — no background noise; (3) background — clean, uncluttered; (4) variety — multiple outfits, angles, lingerie styles, and outdoor locations. This is delivered as part of the onboarding process, not as reactive feedback.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) evaluates *incoming* inspo reels for viral score but there is no documented creator-facing quality brief or production standards SOP. Quality feedback is presumably reactive via the revision workflow (Tasks table).
findings.recommendation: Create a one-page 'Content Quality Standards' PDF/Notion doc covering the four pillars above. Send it during onboarding and reference it in weekly content briefs. Add a quality-gate field to the Assets Airtable table so editors can flag substandard submissions for coaching rather than silently fixing or discarding.

sources.quote: lighting is probably the number one most important thing when it comes to taking high quality content make sure that you're using at least a ring light
make sure that the background of the videos and the pictures and everything doesn't have a bunch like a mess or Garbage

findings.title: Hold weekly video calls with each creator to give account updates and collect feedback
findings.claim: A recurring weekly Zoom/video call with the model — showing results, flagging problems honestly, and requesting content adjustments — is the primary relationship-maintenance mechanism.
findings.what_they_do: They schedule a standing weekly call per model on a video platform (Zoom or equivalent). The call covers: current account performance update (good and bad news), requested content changes for the coming week, and any operational issues. The visual face-to-face element is cited as relationship-strengthening beyond what async messaging achieves.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator success cadence or QBR rhythm (§9 flags this as a gap). Communication is through Telegram per §5. There is no standing weekly call structure with creators.
findings.recommendation: Implement a standing weekly 20-min creator check-in call. Build a lightweight call template in Notion: (1) last-week metrics summary pulled from OF dashboard, (2) content requests for next week, (3) any platform/account issues, (4) open Q&A. Log call notes back to the Creators Airtable record. This directly fills the §9 retention/churn gap.

sources.quote: set up a Cadence of calls like on a weekly basis where you get on a zoom or get on some sort of call with the model you see them face to face and you explain what's going on

findings.title: Use weekly invoicing explicitly as a trust signal, not just a payment mechanism
findings.claim: Consistent invoicing builds model trust because it creates transparency and accountability, making it irrational for the model to leave a reliable agency over a single payment.
findings.what_they_do: They invoice every creator weekly and explain the invoicing schedule during the onboarding call. They frame invoicing not just as revenue collection but as a trust-building tool — a paper trail that shows the agency is accountable. They also argue that a model generating consistent income has no incentive to leave, reducing churn.
findings.vs_us: Palm already auto-generates weekly invoices (§7 — multi-account PDF via Puppeteer, Dropbox, Resend). However, Palm's invoicing system is described as an ops tool, not a trust/relationship tool. There is no documented practice of *explaining* the invoicing schedule during onboarding or positioning it as a transparency signal to the creator.
findings.recommendation: Add an 'invoicing walkthrough' step to the onboarding call checklist: show the creator the invoice format, confirm the payment cadence, and explicitly frame it as mutual accountability. This costs nothing — Palm's invoice infrastructure already exists — but closes the relationship-framing gap.

sources.quote: always invoice your clients it's a way to build trust but they're also not going to run if you provide a quality service

findings.title: Proactively disclose account issues to creators — hiding problems destroys trust faster than the problem itself
findings.claim: Agencies that hide negative account developments lose model trust catastrophically when the model discovers the issue independently; proactive disclosure, even of bad news, strengthens the relationship.
findings.what_they_do: Their agency policy is to give updates on account performance whether good or bad. If something goes wrong (traffic drop, platform issue, content underperformance), they inform the model directly rather than waiting or papering over it. This is presented as a cultural value established from onboarding onwards.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator communications policy around bad news or account issues (§6 thin, §9 no retention strategy). The weekly invoice cadence provides a financial signal but there is no SOP for flagging non-financial account problems to creators.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'bad news protocol' to Palm's creator comms SOP: define what events trigger a proactive creator notification (e.g., >20% subscriber drop, platform flag, traffic source failure) and set a response-time SLA (e.g., notify within 24 hours). Document this protocol and mention it during onboarding so creators know to expect it. Supports §9 retention.

sources.quote: you don't want to just make it seem like everything's going perfectly and them to figure out something's wrong on their own because that's going to be just relationship destroying

findings.title: Use the onboarding process itself as a passive screening tool for creator quality and reliability
findings.claim: How a model responds during onboarding — responsiveness, receptiveness to feedback, implementation of early requests — is a leading indicator of her long-term revenue potential and whether the agency should continue investing in her.
findings.what_they_do: They observe model behavior during the onboarding phase as a quality signal: slow responses, failure to implement feedback, or resistance to criticism are treated as early warnings that the creator will not reach top-tier earnings. They acknowledge that new agencies must work with whoever they can get but advise building judgment over time to screen for quality clients.
findings.vs_us: Palm's onboarding is a form wizard (§6) — it captures data but generates no behavioral signal about the creator. There is no documented creator-quality scoring or early-stage filtering criteria.
findings.recommendation: Add a 30-day post-onboarding creator quality review field to the Creators Airtable table. Score creators on: (1) content submission timeliness, (2) responsiveness on Telegram, (3) implementation of feedback from first weekly call. Use scores to prioritize agency resource allocation and, as the roster scales, to inform future signing decisions.

sources.quote: if they're flaky or they don't respond very good or they take forever to respond or they don't Implement criticism you most likely don't have a very high quality client

findings.title: Never send mass PPVs — individual messaging generates 3-4x more revenue
findings.claim: Mass PPVs lose far more money than they make because the non-buyers (majority) could have been converted through individual, personalized outreach.
findings.what_they_do: They avoid mass PPV blasts entirely. Instead, chatters message each subscriber individually, open with small talk to warm them up, then present content as if it was created specifically for that fan. The argument: of 1,000 subscribers who receive a $20 mass PPV, ~50 buy; but of the 950 who didn't, 100–200 more could be closed through 1-on-1 conversation — revenue that is permanently lost with mass sends.
findings.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal SOP on PPV delivery method. No documented position on mass vs. individual PPV exists. This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
findings.recommendation: When building the in-house chat SOP, institute a hard policy against mass PPV blasts. Default all PPV delivery to individual chat sequences. Use this framing in chatter training: mass PPV = lazy; individual = 10x revenue floor.

sources.quote: if you send mass ppvs yes you will make some money... the problem is you lose way more money than you actually do
it is never and trust me on that never a good idea to send mass ppvs... just chat with them yourself you will make 10 times the money minimum

findings.title: Use a tiered 'script' — escalating PPV ladder from $15 to $200+ per session
findings.claim: A pre-built escalation script that guides the fan through progressively more explicit content at increasing price points generates $300–$500 per subscriber per session vs. a one-off $50 custom video.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters use a fixed script structure: (1) open with small talk / lifestyle warmup, (2) send 1–2 free teaser images to raise arousal, (3) transition to selling with a tiered video ladder — e.g., $15 (tease only) → $30 (slightly more) → $60 (light action) → $120 (more explicit) → $200 (full action). Content explicitness scales with price. Subscriber buys each step sequentially; by the final video, the session is complete and they've spent $300–$500. The same script is reused across all subscribers.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or chat script structure (§7 GAP, §8 OUTSOURCED). No internal baseline exists for escalation sequencing. This is high-value build-toward intel for both the in-house chat team and AI chatting projects.
findings.recommendation: Build a canonical escalation script template for chatters: define 5–6 tiers with explicit price points, content-reveal thresholds, and transition copy at each step. Treat it as a reusable asset deployed across all creators. Price anchoring should start low ($15) to minimize entry friction. Prioritize this for the AI chatting project where scripts can be fully automated.

sources.quote: you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200... you just continuously progress
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber and that's how your channel goes to the big big levels

findings.topic: Emotional experience framing vs. transactional selling
findings.title: Sell the intimate experience, not the content — fans pay for emotion, not pornography
findings.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal script or framing doctrine. This 'experience over transaction' principle should anchor chatter training philosophy when Palm builds in-house. Also directly applicable to AI chatting — arguably more so, since AI chatters must compensate for lack of a real person.
findings.recommendation: Make 'sell the experience, not the content' the #1 principle in the chatter training SOP. Require chatters to spend a defined minimum number of exchanges on warmup/small talk before any PPV offer. For the AI chatting project, build emotional escalation cues into the conversation state machine — track where in the 'intimacy arc' each fan is before triggering a sell.

sources.quote: only fans isn't about sexuality it's about emotions and experiences
what he really searches for are the emotions the real sexual experience not just the pure sexuality

findings.topic: Free teaser content as pre-sell warm-up
findings.title: Send 1–2 free teaser images before any PPV offer to prime the subscriber
findings.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
findings.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.
findings.vs_us: No internal chat SOP exists at Palm (§8). This specific 'free teaser before first pitch' mechanic is a discrete, concrete tactic to encode into the future chatter SOP and AI chatting state machine.
findings.recommendation: Define a mandatory 'warm-up phase' in the chat SOP: 1 free teaser image sent before any PPV offer, followed by a reaction-check message. Only proceed to the $15 entry PPV after a positive subscriber response. This applies directly to AI chatting — build the state machine so it cannot enter 'sell mode' before completing the warm-up phase.

sources.quote: I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more

findings.title: Anchor the PPV ladder at $15 entry point to minimize first-purchase friction
findings.claim: Starting the PPV ladder at a low price ($15) minimizes the psychological barrier to the first purchase, making it far more likely the subscriber enters the escalation funnel and ultimately spends $300–$500.
findings.what_they_do: The specific ladder cited: $15 → $30 → $60 → $120 → $200 → $200. Entry price is low enough to feel trivial. Each step roughly doubles, creating anchoring where each subsequent price feels proportional to the last rather than shocking. The final $200 items are 'full action' — maximum explicitness justifying maximum price. Total session value: ~$625 at full conversion.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 GAP — identified as #1 creator concern). This is the clearest external data point available for building Palm's first PPV pricing SOP. The doubling structure is a concrete starting model.
findings.recommendation: Adopt this ladder structure as the baseline for Palm's PPV pricing SOP: 6 tiers starting at $15, roughly doubling each step. Document it formally in §7 pricing docs. Test against the external chat team's current pricing to see if a ladder approach could increase per-fan revenue. This also gives Palm a concrete pricing framework to share with creators during onboarding (addressing the #1 creator concern).

sources.quote: you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200 the next one for 200
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber

findings.title: One reusable script deployed across all subscribers = scalable chat ops
findings.claim: A single well-constructed escalation script can be reused with every subscriber, making chat operations scalable without requiring chatters to freestyle or produce new custom content daily.
findings.what_they_do: The script is treated as a static, reusable asset — not a custom conversation. Chatters are trained to deliver it as if it's personal and spontaneous (the 'experience' framing), but operationally it's the same sequence for every fan. This eliminates the content-production bottleneck of true custom content (where a new video must be filmed for each request) and lets chatters handle higher fan volume with consistent outcomes.
findings.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal script assets. This 'script as reusable product' model is directly applicable to the AI chatting roadmap project — the state machine should implement the script once and deploy it across all fans on a given creator's account.
findings.recommendation: For the AI chatting project, build the escalation script as a canonical, versioned asset per creator (not per conversation). Track which 'step' in the script each fan has reached using Fan Tracker CRM (§9) so the AI resumes at the correct point. For the in-house chat team, train chatters on script delivery as a skill (making canned feel personal) rather than improv.

sources.quote: it's easy to replicate you just have one script ready and you can sell it to every single subscriber
ideally the subscriber gets more and more horny he likes the script more and more he buys everything

findings.title: Package VA chatter training as a zipped module with persona PDF
findings.claim: Sending a structured, zipped training module to new chatter VAs — including a persona guide, payment info, and scripts — accelerates onboarding and ensures consistency.
findings.what_they_do: When hiring a chatter VA, Bjorn compiles a ZIP package containing: (1) a PDF guide on the model's persona (age range, niche, typing style, pet names, emojis), (2) payment/platform instructions, (3) a pre-written script. The VA unzips and self-trains before starting.
findings.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting to an external team (§8) with no documented internal SOP or onboarding material. There is no equivalent persona brief or chatter training kit. This is a direct gap to fill as Palm builds toward an in-house chat team.
findings.recommendation: As Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), create a per-creator ZIP/Notion training module template: persona card (age, niche, tone, emojis, pet names), platform walkthrough, PPV pricing ladder, and a baseline script. Standardize this as the chatter onboarding artifact.

sources.quote: some training material that I prepare for our VAs so when I hire a VA for the purposes of chatting I'll input the necessary information from the model in this guide here zip it up along with a few other things and then send it to the VA the chatter as a training module

findings.title: Brief chatters on model persona: tone, emojis, typing quirks, niche
findings.claim: Chatters must mirror the model's real personality and typing style (excessive letters, cute emojis, pet names) so subscribers cannot detect a VA is behind the keyboard.
findings.what_they_do: The persona section of the training PDF specifies: age range of the model → inferred typing style (e.g. 18–24 = lots of cute emojis, elongated words like 'heyyyy'), the model's content niche, any pet names she uses, and other personality markers. Chatter applies all of these verbatim.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) and there is no documented persona-briefing process internally. When Palm builds in-house or AI chatting, voice consistency will be critical — especially for AI personas (§3) where breaking character destroys the illusion.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'chatter voice card' field to the Creators table in Airtable (and AI Account Profile for AI personas). Populate: age, typing quirks, signature emojis, pet names, niche. Feed this card to any chatter (human or AI) at session start. Especially high priority for AI persona chatting.

sources.quote: take note of the client's Persona so if the girl is aged between say 18 to 24 then using a lot of cute emojis when typing using a lot of excessive letters in some words

findings.title: Price PPVs at .99 endings to exploit charm pricing psychology
findings.claim: Pricing PPV content at $X.99 instead of round numbers (e.g. $49.99 vs $50) makes content feel meaningfully cheaper and increases purchase rates.
findings.what_they_do: Any PPV intended to sell at a round number is repriced to one cent below: $50 → $49.99, $35 → $34.99. This is presented as a direct borrowing of supermarket/retail charm-pricing psychology and is codified as a standing rule in the chatter training guide.
findings.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing model is documented at Palm (§7 GAP). There is no codified rule on round vs. charm pricing. This is a quick, zero-cost win to adopt.
findings.recommendation: Add a pricing rule to the future PPV pricing SOP: all PPV prices must end in .99 (never round numbers). Apply immediately when Palm's in-house chat team is stood up, and bake into any AI chatting prompt templates.

sources.quote: avoid whole numbers when pricing PPVs so if you plan to sell a piece of content for 50 then you'll just reprice it and sell it for $49.99

findings.title: Start subscribers on $15–$20 PPVs before escalating to $80–$100+
findings.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
findings.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or sequencing rules (§7 GAP, §8 outsourced). The external chat team may do this implicitly, but it is not a formalized Palm playbook. This is core build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat roadmap.
findings.recommendation: Define a 3-tier PPV ladder in the chatter SOP: Tier 1 (entry, $15–$20) → Tier 2 (mid, $30–$50) → Tier 3 (high-ticket, $80–$100+). Require at least 2 Tier 1 purchases before a Tier 3 offer. Encode this sequencing into AI chatting logic when that project advances.

sources.quote: you'll start off selling subscriber content from $15 to $20 so it's kind of like dipping into the water seeing where they're at and then after the subscribers purchase multiple pieces you can start selling PPVs for high ticket prices like 80 bucks 90 100

findings.title: Always wrap up a sexting session before logging off to prevent churn
findings.claim: Abruptly ending a chat session after a subscriber has spent money makes them feel used and dramatically reduces the chance they return or spend again.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters are explicitly instructed to close every sexting/monetization session with a proper conversational wrap-up — warm sign-off, leaving the subscriber feeling positive and wanting to return. Logging off mid-session is a fireable-level error in the VA training.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) with no internal SOP. This 'session closure' practice is a key retention mechanic that should be codified in Palm's future in-house chatter SOP and AI chatting prompt instructions.
findings.recommendation: Add 'session closure protocol' to the chatter SOP: before ending any active conversation, send a warm closing message (e.g. affirmation, teaser for next time). Make this a scored QA checkpoint when reviewing chatter performance.

sources.quote: always wrap up the conversation sexting session before logging off this is to leave the subscriber feeling good and wanting to come back for more next time otherwise if you abruptly stop replying the subscriber has spent hundreds of dollars on content they're going to feel used

findings.title: Send a single sad-face emoji to re-engage non-responding subscribers
findings.claim: Sending a lone sad-face emoji to a subscriber who has gone quiet triggers guilt and reliably provokes a response, re-opening the monetization window.
findings.what_they_do: When a subscriber has not replied to a previous message, instead of sending follow-up texts or more content pitches, the chatter sends only a '😢' or '🥺' emoji. Bjorn claims ~99.9% response rate. The mechanism: guilt-trips the subscriber into explaining their absence, which restarts rapport and spending. No spamming — one emoji only.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented re-engagement tactic (§8 outsourced, §9 partial fan retention). This is a high-signal, zero-cost tactic for the in-house chat roadmap and is also relevant to AI chatting logic (trigger sad emoji after N hours of non-response).
findings.recommendation: Build this into the chatter re-engagement SOP: if subscriber has not replied after 24–48 hours, send a single sad-face emoji (no text). Log the response rate in Fan Tracker CRM to validate. For AI chatting, add as an automated trigger: no-reply after configurable timeout → send sad emoji → await response before any further action.

sources.quote: if a subscriber has not replied to your previous message and you want to get his attention simply just send a sad face emoji it is amazing how this will work I would say this probably works 99.9% of the time

findings.title: Never send multiple follow-up messages to a non-responding subscriber
findings.claim: Sending multiple unanswered messages in a row appears overwhelming and spammy, actively pushing subscribers away and reducing future engagement.
findings.what_they_do: The VA training guide explicitly prohibits sending additional messages if a subscriber has not responded. One message sits unanswered → chatter holds. The only permitted follow-up is the sad-face emoji tactic (single message). This is a firm SOP rule, not a suggestion.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no internal chat SOP (§8 outsourced). If the external team lacks this discipline it may be causing churn Palm can't see. This rule should be a core constraint in both the in-house chatter SOP and AI chatting prompt guardrails.
findings.recommendation: Add a hard rule to the chatter SOP and AI chatting system prompt: after one unanswered message, no further text messages until the subscriber replies (only the sad-face emoji exception is permitted). Make this a QA audit criterion when reviewing chatter logs.

sources.quote: do not spam subscribers with messages if they're not responding try not to send additional messages if the sub is not responding as this can appear overwhelming and spammy and could potentially put the subscriber off

findings.title: Replace rule-sets with 4–5 core values to govern chatter behavior
findings.claim: Values (4–5 core beliefs) outperform rule-sets because they apply to every situation, are easier to enforce, and create a positive work environment.
findings.what_they_do: Agency defines 4–5 values instead of an exhaustive rule list: (1) work for creators / act in their best interest even if it costs money; (2) everyone spends — treat every fan as a potential buyer; (3) make your opportunities — proactively go to fans; (4) play for the team — think agency-wide, not just personal earnings. When a chatter's decision is questioned, the manager asks 'how did that align with our values?' rather than citing a broken rule.
findings.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced to an external chatting team (§8); no internal values framework or SOP exists. This is build-toward intelligence for the planned in-house chat team (~1yr out).
findings.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, document 4–5 agency values as the governance layer before writing any procedural rules. Draft values now so they're ready for onboarding the first internal chatter.

sources.quote: Four to five core beliefs that you want instilled in your chatting team

findings.title: Set expectations via call + concise video/docs emphasizing common beginner mistakes first
findings.claim: New chatters must be shown explicitly what 'good' looks like via a call plus a short video and documents that lead with the most common beginner errors before anything else.
findings.what_they_do: Run an onboarding call with the new chatter; share a pre-recorded video and 2–3 concise documents. Documents are structured around the most common mistakes (dry replies that kill conversation, overly aggressive/obvious PPV transitions) and use side-by-side examples — 'wrong version' vs 'right version' — for carrying conversations, captioning PPVs, transitioning, etc.
findings.vs_us: No internal chatter onboarding materials exist at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house team.
findings.recommendation: Create a 'chatter starter kit': a ≤10-min Loom walkthrough + a 1-page doc listing top 5 beginner mistakes with example chat screenshots. Prioritize mistake-first framing over comprehensive rules.

sources.quote: We put emphasis on common beginner mistakes
examples are king in your documents. Just show this is an example of a chatter doing it wrong. This is an example of a chatter doing it right

findings.title: Live-call training with seconds-fast feedback loops and single-point focus
findings.claim: The fastest way to improve a new chatter is to sit on a live call with them while they chat, give feedback within seconds, and focus on fixing only one thing at a time.
findings.what_they_do: Manager joins a live call with the chatter while they work in real time. Feedback is immediate ('stop, do it again'). Manager identifies the single highest-leverage improvement point and works only on that until fixed, then moves to the next. Chatters are not 'saved' — manager tells them what to do but makes them execute it themselves so the learning sticks.
findings.vs_us: No internal chatter training exists (§8, outsourced). Build-toward.
findings.recommendation: Adopt this as the core training protocol for first 2–4 weeks of any new in-house chatter. Build a simple tracker: one 'current focus point' per chatter updated weekly so managers don't multi-task the coaching.

sources.quote: Fast feedback loops. If you want someone to learn something quickly, you need there to be a short gap between when they do something and when they get feedback on it. And I mean seconds
Don't step in and do it for them. You can tell them what to do. That's how they actually learn

findings.title: Weekly async Loom chat reviews focused on new subscribers + high-ticket purchases
findings.claim: Regular async video reviews (via Loom) of specific conversation types — new subscribers, purchases, high-ticket sales — plus chatter-submitted chats drive measurable skill improvement.
findings.what_they_do: Manager opens the 'new subscribers' tab and reviews those conversations each week. Also reviews purchase logs and high-ticket sales. Asks each chatter to submit specific chats for review. Records Loom feedback videos. Per-review focus stays on one improvement point. Maintains 'scientist mode' (critical, constructive) not 'dictator mode' (anger-driven). Each chatter gets one weekly improvement point plus actionable steps.
findings.vs_us: No internal review cadence at Palm (§8, outsourced). Partially relevant to the AI chatting project as well. Build-toward.
findings.recommendation: Design a weekly review ritual into the in-house chat SOP from day one: manager records a ≤5-min Loom per chatter, logs the single focus point and action steps in Airtable (add a 'Chatter Reviews' table under the chat team's workspace).

sources.quote: We open up the new subscribers tab and read those conversations. We look at purchases. We look for high ticket sales. And we ask chatters every week to give us specific chats to review
Every week we analyze the one point of improvement for each chatter

findings.title: On-shift group call + peer-authority system accelerates collective chatter improvement
findings.claim: An open call for all chatters currently on shift — combined with assigning subject-matter 'authorities' among chatters — creates collaborative learning that makes the whole team improve faster than top-down coaching alone.
findings.what_they_do: Maintain an always-open call channel for chatters on the same shift so they can ask each other for help and share mistakes in real time. Identify each chatter's main strength (e.g., scripting, PPV captioning). When another chatter needs help in that area, route them to the peer expert rather than the manager. This builds internal figures of authority and reduces manager bottleneck.
findings.vs_us: No internal chat team structure at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house team. Relevant also to AI chatting project's team design.
findings.recommendation: When standing up the in-house chat team, create a Telegram or Discord group call as an 'on-shift room.' Map each chatter's strongest skill in Airtable and use that routing table to build peer mentorship from week one.

sources.quote: having an on shift call where the chatters who are currently working can talk to each other
Chatters tend to have one main strength. Identify what that is and then whenever a different chatter asks for help on that topic, you send them to that chatter

findings.title: Daily end-of-shift written takeaways lock in incremental chatter learning
findings.claim: Requiring chatters to record written takeaways at the end of every shift — specifically one actionable improvement for the next shift — accelerates skill development during the first month.
findings.what_they_do: For roughly the first month, each chatter writes a brief end-of-shift note answering: 'What are the actionable steps I'll take next shift to improve on what I did this shift?' This creates a self-directed feedback loop between supervised live-training sessions.
findings.vs_us: No internal chatter development loop at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward.
findings.recommendation: Include a daily takeaway template in the chatter onboarding kit. A simple Airtable or Notion form ('shift date / one thing to improve / how I'll do it differently') submitted at shift-end gives managers a lightweight pulse without requiring constant live supervision.

sources.quote: For the first month or so, get the chatters to record their daily takeaways. At the end of each shift, okay, what are the actionable steps you're going to take next shift
